name: Gödel's First Incompleteness Theorem
statement: Any consistent formal system capable of expressing basic arithmetic contains true statements that cannot be proved within the system.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(axioms + inference rules) → COMPLETE(all arithmetic truths) FAILS → self-referential sentence G encodes 'I am unprovable', forcing incompleteness
known_resolutions: Add the unprovable statement as a new axiom (creates new unprovable statements)
Move to stronger systems (ordinal analysis)
Accept incompleteness as intrinsic to sufficiently expressive systems

name: Gödel's Second Incompleteness Theorem
statement: No consistent formal system capable of expressing basic arithmetic can prove its own consistency.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(axioms + proof system) → COMPLETE(self-consistency proof) FAILS → consistency statement implies unprovable sentence, creating circularity
known_resolutions: Prove consistency in a stronger system (Gentzen's proof of PA consistency in PRA + transfinite induction)
Relative consistency proofs
Finitistic reductions

name: Tarski's Undefinability of Truth
statement: No sufficiently expressive formal language can define its own truth predicate. There is no formula True(x) in the language of arithmetic that correctly classifies all sentences as true or false.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(language + self-reference) → COMPLETE(truth predicate for own sentences) FAILS → Liar-type paradox forces inconsistency or incompleteness of the predicate
source: Tarski (1936), 'The Concept of Truth in Formalized Languages'
known_resolutions: Stratified truth predicates (Kripke's fixed-point theory)
Tarskian hierarchy of metalanguages
Paraconsistent logics that tolerate contradiction

name: Church's Undecidability of First-Order Logic
statement: There is no algorithm that can determine whether an arbitrary first-order logic sentence is valid (a tautology).
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(first-order sentences + decision procedure) → COMPLETE(validity oracle) FAILS → reduction from halting problem proves no such algorithm exists
known_resolutions: Semi-decision procedures (enumerate all proofs)
Restrict to decidable fragments (monadic, two-variable, guarded)
Herbrand's theorem for systematic proof search

name: Undecidability of the Halting Problem
statement: There is no Turing machine that can determine, for every program-input pair, whether the program halts or runs forever.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(program + input + decider) → COMPLETE(halt/loop classification) FAILS → diagonalization: decider applied to itself yields contradiction
known_resolutions: Restrict to decidable subclasses (primitive recursive, structured programs)
Timeouts and approximations
Rice's theorem generalizes: all non-trivial semantic properties are undecidable

name: Rice's Theorem
statement: Every non-trivial semantic property of programs (partial functions) is undecidable. No algorithm can determine whether an arbitrary program has any given non-trivial property of its input-output behavior.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(programs + semantic property test) → COMPLETE(classification of all programs) FAILS → reduction from halting problem for any non-trivial property
source: Rice (1953), 'Classes of Recursively Enumerable Sets and Their Decision Problems'
known_resolutions: Syntactic over-approximations (abstract interpretation)
Restrict to finite-state or decidable program classes
Probabilistic or heuristic analysis

name: Independence of the Continuum Hypothesis
statement: The Continuum Hypothesis (no set has cardinality strictly between aleph_0 and 2^aleph_0) can neither be proved nor refuted from the standard ZFC axioms.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(ZFC axioms) → COMPLETE(determination of cardinal arithmetic) FAILS → forcing (Cohen) and inner models (Gödel) show both CH and ¬CH are consistent with ZFC
source: Gödel (1940) consistency, Cohen (1963) independence via forcing
known_resolutions: Add large cardinal axioms or determinacy axioms
Adopt V=L (implies CH)
Forcing axioms like Martin's Axiom or PFA (imply ¬CH)
Multiverse view: both are valid in different set-theoretic universes

name: Impossibility of Angle Trisection
statement: It is impossible to trisect an arbitrary angle using only compass and straightedge in a finite number of steps. Specifically, a 60° angle cannot be trisected because cos(20°) is a root of 8x³-6x-1=0, which is irreducible over Q.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(compass + straightedge operations) → COMPLETE(trisection of arbitrary angle) FAILS → constructible numbers form a tower of degree-2 extensions; trisection requires degree-3 extension
source: Wantzel (1837), proved via Galois theory / field extensions
known_resolutions: Neusis construction (marked ruler)
Origami (Huzita-Hatori axioms solve all cubics)
Trisectrix curves
Tomahawk mechanical device

name: Impossibility of Doubling the Cube
statement: It is impossible to construct a cube with exactly twice the volume of a given cube using only compass and straightedge. This requires constructing the cube root of 2, which has minimal polynomial x³-2 of degree 3 over Q.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(compass + straightedge) → COMPLETE(cube root of 2) FAILS → constructible field extensions have degree 2^n; degree 3 is not a power of 2
known_resolutions: Neusis construction
Conic section intersections (Menaechmus)
Origami folds
Mechanical linkages

name: Impossibility of Squaring the Circle
statement: It is impossible to construct a square with the same area as a given circle using only compass and straightedge, because π is transcendental and therefore not constructible.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(compass + straightedge) → COMPLETE(√π as constructible length) FAILS → π transcendental (Lindemann 1882) implies √π is transcendental, not in any algebraic extension of Q
source: Lindemann (1882), building on Hermite's proof of transcendence of e
known_resolutions: Approximate constructions (Ramanujan's, Kochanski's)
Quadratrix of Hippias
Infinite compass-straightedge procedures converging to π

name: Gauss-Wantzel Theorem on Constructible Polygons
statement: A regular n-gon is constructible with compass and straightedge if and only if n is a product of a power of 2 and distinct Fermat primes. In particular, regular 7-gon, 9-gon, 11-gon, etc. are impossible.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(compass + straightedge) → COMPLETE(regular n-gon for arbitrary n) FAILS → constructibility requires φ(n) to be a power of 2, constraining n to Gauss-Wantzel form
known_resolutions: Approximate constructions
Neusis or origami for some cases
Mechanical curve-drawing devices

name: Brouwer Fixed Point Theorem (as impossibility)
statement: There is no continuous retraction from the closed n-disk to its boundary n-1 sphere. Equivalently, every continuous map from the closed n-disk to itself has a fixed point.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(continuous map on disk) → COMPLETE(avoid all fixed points) FAILS → retraction would induce identity on homology of boundary, contradicting triviality of disk homology
known_resolutions: Allow discontinuous maps
Use non-convex or non-compact domains
Approximate fixed points (Sperner's lemma gives constructive version)

name: Hairy Ball Theorem
statement: There is no continuous non-vanishing tangent vector field on even-dimensional spheres S^(2n). Every continuous tangent vector field on S² must vanish at some point.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(continuous tangent vectors on S²) → COMPLETE(everywhere non-zero field) FAILS → Euler characteristic χ(S²)=2 ≠ 0 forces a zero by Poincaré-Hopf theorem
known_resolutions: Odd-dimensional spheres admit non-vanishing fields (S¹, S³, S⁷)
Allow isolated zeros and study their indices
Use line fields instead of vector fields

name: No Retraction Theorem
statement: There is no continuous retraction from the n-disk D^n onto its boundary S^(n-1). No continuous map r: D^n → S^(n-1) can satisfy r(x)=x for all x on S^(n-1).
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(continuous map D^n → S^(n-1) + boundary condition) → COMPLETE(retraction) FAILS → induced homology map would factor identity through trivial group
source: Brouwer (1911), equivalent to Brouwer Fixed Point Theorem
known_resolutions: Allow discontinuities
Map to lower-dimensional spheres
Deformation retracts onto subsets that are not the full boundary

name: Invariance of Domain / Dimension
statement: R^m and R^n are not homeomorphic when m ≠ n. There is no continuous bijection with continuous inverse between Euclidean spaces of different dimensions.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(continuous bijection R^m → R^n for m≠n) → COMPLETE(homeomorphism) FAILS → homology groups H_k distinguish dimensions; removing a point yields different homotopy types
known_resolutions: Space-filling curves give continuous surjections (not injections)
Fractal dimensions allow non-integer dimensional concepts
Topological dimension theory formalizes the obstruction

name: Non-extendability of Jordan Curve Theorem to Higher Dimensions (Alexander Horned Sphere)
statement: The Schoenflies theorem (every Jordan curve bounds a disk) does not generalize naively to dimension 3. There exist wild embeddings of S² in R³ where the exterior is not simply connected, so no homeomorphism of R³ maps the embedded sphere to the standard sphere.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(embedded 2-sphere in R³) → COMPLETE(topologically standard embedding) FAILS → Alexander horned sphere creates non-simply-connected exterior despite topological embedding
known_resolutions: Require locally flat (tame) embeddings
PL or smooth category restores Schoenflies
Mazur's theorem for locally flat embeddings in higher dimensions

name: Banach-Tarski Paradox
statement: A solid ball in R³ can be decomposed into finitely many pieces and reassembled (via rotations and translations) into two balls identical to the original. Equivalently, there is no finitely additive, isometry-invariant measure on all subsets of R³ that normalizes the unit ball.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(finitely additive measure + isometry invariance + all subsets of R³) → COMPLETE(consistent volume) FAILS → free group in SO(3) enables paradoxical decomposition via Axiom of Choice
known_resolutions: Restrict to Lebesgue measurable sets
Work in R¹ or R² (amenable groups prevent paradox)
Reject Axiom of Choice (Solovay model)
Accept non-measurable sets as pathological

name: Vitali's Non-Measurable Set
statement: Assuming the Axiom of Choice, there exist subsets of the real line that are not Lebesgue measurable. No translation-invariant, countably additive measure extending length on intervals can be defined on all subsets of R.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(countable additivity + translation invariance + all subsets of R) → COMPLETE(total Lebesgue measure) FAILS → Vitali set constructed via AC from R/Q coset representatives leads to contradiction with σ-additivity
known_resolutions: Restrict to Lebesgue measurable sets (sufficient for analysis)
Solovay model: ZF + DC where all sets are measurable
Use finitely additive measures (Banach limits)

name: Cantor's Uncountability of the Reals
statement: There is no bijection between the natural numbers and the real numbers. The power set of any set has strictly greater cardinality than the set itself (Cantor's theorem).
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(enumeration of reals as a list) → COMPLETE(surjection N → R) FAILS → diagonal argument constructs a real not in any proposed enumeration
known_resolutions: Accept cardinal hierarchy as fundamental
Constructive mathematics limits the scope
Skolem's paradox: countable models of set theory exist but 'uncountable' is relative

name: Transcendence of e and π (Hermite-Lindemann)
statement: The numbers e and π are transcendental: they are not roots of any polynomial with rational coefficients. More generally, if α is a nonzero algebraic number, then e^α is transcendental (Lindemann-Weierstrass).
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(polynomial with rational coefficients) → COMPLETE(root equal to e or π) FAILS → Lindemann-Weierstrass theorem: e^α transcendental for algebraic α≠0; e^(iπ)=-1 forces π transcendental
source: Hermite (1873) for e, Lindemann (1882) for π, Weierstrass (1885) generalization
known_resolutions: Algebraic approximations (continued fractions, Padé approximants)
Irrationality measures quantify 'how transcendental'
Periods framework (Kontsevich-Zagier) classifies transcendental constants

name: Fermat's Last Theorem
statement: There are no positive integer solutions to x^n + y^n = z^n for any integer n ≥ 3. The Fermat equation has no non-trivial solutions in positive integers for exponent 3 or higher.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(positive integers x,y,z with n≥3) → COMPLETE(x^n + y^n = z^n) FAILS → modularity theorem shows the Frey curve cannot be modular and semistable simultaneously
source: Fermat (1637 conjecture), Wiles (1995 proof) via modularity of elliptic curves
known_resolutions: Solutions exist for n=2 (Pythagorean triples)
Allow rational or p-adic solutions
Generalize to Fermat-Catalan conjecture and abc conjecture

name: Irrationality of √2
statement: The square root of 2 cannot be expressed as a ratio of two integers. No fraction p/q in lowest terms satisfies p²=2q².
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(integers p,q) → COMPLETE(p/q = √2) FAILS → infinite descent: if p²=2q² then both p,q must be even, contradicting lowest terms
source: Attributed to Hippasus (5th century BC), proof by contradiction
known_resolutions: Continued fraction expansion gives best rational approximations
p-adic completions provide alternative number systems
Algebraic number field Q(√2) extends rationals

name: Legendre's Three-Square Theorem (impossibility form)
statement: A positive integer n cannot be expressed as the sum of three squares of integers if and only if n is of the form 4^a(8b+7). These numbers provably require four squares.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(three integer squares) → COMPLETE(sum = 4^a(8b+7)) FAILS → quadratic residue mod 8 obstruction: three squares mod 8 can only reach {0,1,2,3,4,5,6}, never 7
source: Legendre (1798), Gauss gave the first complete proof
known_resolutions: Lagrange's four-square theorem: every positive integer is a sum of four squares
Waring's problem generalizes to higher powers
p-adic Hasse-Minkowski theorem gives local-global principle

name: Liouville's Approximation Theorem / Roth's Theorem
statement: Algebraic numbers of degree n cannot be approximated by rationals p/q better than |α - p/q| > c/q^n (Liouville) or c/q^(2+ε) for any ε>0 (Roth). This makes it impossible for certain rapidly-converging sequences of rationals to converge to an algebraic number.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(rational approximations p_k/q_k converging too fast) → COMPLETE(limit is algebraic) FAILS → algebraic numbers resist super-polynomial rational approximation
source: Liouville (1844), Thue (1909), Siegel (1921), Roth (1955, Fields Medal)
known_resolutions: Liouville numbers are explicitly transcendental by this criterion
Schmidt's subspace theorem generalizes to simultaneous approximation
Effective bounds remain a major open problem

name: Failure of Local-Global Principle (Hasse Principle)
statement: The local-global principle fails for general varieties: a Diophantine equation can have solutions in every p-adic field Q_p and in R yet have no rational solution. The first example is Selmer's curve 3x³+4y³+5z³=0.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(solutions over all local fields Q_p, R) → COMPLETE(global rational solution) FAILS → Brauer-Manin obstruction blocks the local-to-global passage
known_resolutions: Brauer-Manin obstruction explains many failures
Hasse principle holds for quadratic forms (Hasse-Minkowski theorem)
Descent methods and Selmer groups quantify the failure

name: Matiyasevich's Theorem (Hilbert's 10th Problem)
statement: There is no algorithm to determine whether an arbitrary polynomial Diophantine equation with integer coefficients has an integer solution.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(Diophantine equation + decision algorithm) → COMPLETE(solvability oracle) FAILS → every recursively enumerable set is Diophantine, reducing halting problem to Diophantine solvability
source: Matiyasevich (1970), building on Davis, Putnam, Robinson (DPRM theorem)
known_resolutions: Decidable for single-variable polynomials
Decidable for linear Diophantine equations
Open for rationals (Hilbert's 10th over Q)
Heuristic and probabilistic methods

name: Arrow's Impossibility Theorem
statement: No ranked voting system for three or more candidates can simultaneously satisfy unrestricted domain, Pareto efficiency, independence of irrelevant alternatives, and non-dictatorship.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(fairness axioms for social choice) → COMPLETE(consistent aggregation of preferences) FAILS → ultrafilter argument shows decisive sets must be singletons (dictator)
known_resolutions: Weaken IIA (Borda count)
Allow cardinal utilities (approval voting)
Restrict preference domains (single-peaked preferences)
Probabilistic or randomized mechanisms

name: Ramsey's Theorem (impossibility of total disorder)
statement: For any positive integers r and s, there exists a minimum integer R(r,s) such that any 2-coloring of the edges of K_{R(r,s)} must contain a monochromatic K_r or K_s. Complete disorder is impossible in sufficiently large structures.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(2-coloring of large complete graph) → COMPLETE(avoidance of all monochromatic cliques) FAILS → pigeonhole principle forces monochromatic substructure at sufficient scale
source: Ramsey (1930), 'On a Problem of Formal Logic'
known_resolutions: Explicit Ramsey numbers known only for small cases
Probabilistic method gives existence bounds
Hales-Jewett theorem and Szemerédi's theorem extend to other contexts

name: Non-existence of 3-coloring for Arbitrary Planar Graphs
statement: Not every planar graph can be properly 3-colored (Grötzsch's theorem gives 3-colorability only for triangle-free planar graphs). There exist planar graphs requiring 4 colors, and the Four Color Theorem shows 4 always suffices.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(3 colors + arbitrary planar graph) → COMPLETE(proper coloring) FAILS → small planar graphs with triangles force chromatic number 4
source: Appel & Haken (1976), Four Color Theorem; Grötzsch (1959) for triangle-free case
known_resolutions: Four colors always suffice (Four Color Theorem)
Three colors suffice for triangle-free planar graphs
List coloring and choosability generalizations

name: Baire Category Theorem (impossibility of meager decomposition)
statement: A complete metric space cannot be expressed as a countable union of nowhere dense sets. The real line is not meager (of first category) in itself.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(countably many nowhere dense sets) → COMPLETE(cover entire complete metric space) FAILS → intersection of countably many dense open sets is still dense in a complete metric space
known_resolutions: Generic properties hold on a residual (comeager) set
Banach-Mazur game gives game-theoretic interpretation
Used to prove existence results non-constructively (generic continuous functions are nowhere differentiable)

name: Existence of Continuous Nowhere-Differentiable Functions
statement: Continuity does not imply differentiability: there exist functions that are continuous everywhere but differentiable nowhere. Moreover, the 'generic' continuous function (in the Baire category sense) is nowhere differentiable.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(continuous function on [0,1]) → COMPLETE(differentiability at even one point) FAILS → Weierstrass function and Baire category argument show differentiable functions are meager in C[0,1]
known_resolutions: Lipschitz conditions guarantee almost-everywhere differentiability (Rademacher)
Sobolev spaces provide weak differentiability
Fractal analysis characterizes the irregular behavior

name: Failure of Pointwise Convergence of Fourier Series
statement: There exist continuous functions whose Fourier series diverges at a point. The Fourier series of a general L¹ function may diverge almost everywhere (Kolmogorov). Uniform convergence of Fourier series requires stronger regularity than mere continuity.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(Fourier partial sums of continuous function) → COMPLETE(pointwise convergence everywhere) FAILS → du Bois-Reymond: unbounded Dirichlet kernel creates divergence at a point; Carleson: L² convergence a.e. is the best general result
source: du Bois-Reymond (1876) divergence for continuous f; Kolmogorov (1926) a.e. divergence for L¹; Carleson (1966) a.e. convergence for L²
known_resolutions: Fejér means (Cesàro summation) converge uniformly for continuous functions
L² functions converge a.e. (Carleson-Hunt theorem)
Dini/Dirichlet conditions give pointwise convergence

name: No Continuous Bijection R→R² (Netto's Theorem)
statement: There is no continuous bijection from R to R^n for n≥2. While space-filling curves (Peano, Hilbert) are continuous surjections R→R², they are never injective.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(continuous map R → R²) → COMPLETE(bijection) FAILS → removing a point from R disconnects it; continuous image must preserve this topological property, preventing bijection with R²
source: Netto (1879), proved rigorously by Lüroth and others
known_resolutions: Continuous surjections exist (space-filling curves)
Measurable bijections exist (Borel isomorphism theorem)
Homeomorphisms between fractals of matching dimension

name: Runge's Phenomenon
statement: Polynomial interpolation at equally spaced points can diverge as the degree increases. For f(x)=1/(1+25x²) on [-1,1], equally-spaced interpolants diverge at the boundary despite f being infinitely smooth.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(equidistant interpolation nodes + increasing degree) → COMPLETE(convergent polynomial approximation) FAILS → Lebesgue constant grows exponentially for equidistant nodes, amplifying oscillations
known_resolutions: Chebyshev nodes minimize Lebesgue constant (near-optimal convergence)
Spline interpolation avoids high-degree polynomials
Least-squares or regularized approximation

name: Müntz-Szász Theorem (incompleteness of lacunary powers)
statement: The span of {x^{λ_n}} is dense in C[0,1] if and only if Σ 1/λ_n = ∞ (with λ_n > 0 distinct). If the exponents are too sparse (series converges), no polynomial combination of those powers can approximate arbitrary continuous functions.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(lacunary set of power functions x^{λ_n}) → COMPLETE(dense subspace of C[0,1]) FAILS → when Σ1/λ_n < ∞, the closed span misses functions in C[0,1]
known_resolutions: Full polynomial basis (Weierstrass approximation)
Add enough exponents to make the sum diverge
Use different basis systems (wavelets, splines)

name: Well-Ordering Requires Axiom of Choice
statement: Without the Axiom of Choice, it is consistent that the real numbers cannot be well-ordered. The Well-Ordering Theorem is equivalent to the Axiom of Choice.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(ZF axioms without Choice) → COMPLETE(well-ordering of R) FAILS → Cohen's forcing shows models of ZF exist where R has no well-ordering
known_resolutions: Accept Axiom of Choice (standard in ZFC)
Use Zorn's Lemma (equivalent)
Work in constructive mathematics with dependent choice only

name: Löwenheim-Skolem Theorem (impossibility of categorical first-order theories)
statement: No first-order theory with an infinite model is categorical: if it has a model of any infinite cardinality, it has models of all sufficiently large cardinalities. First-order logic cannot pin down the cardinality of infinite structures.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(first-order axioms) → COMPLETE(unique infinite model up to isomorphism) FAILS → compactness and Löwenheim-Skolem produce models of every infinite cardinality
known_resolutions: Second-order logic can be categorical (but loses completeness)
Morley's categoricity theorem: categorical in one uncountable cardinal implies categorical in all
Add infinitary logic or generalized quantifiers

name: Impossibility of Convex Pentagonal Lattice Tiling
statement: There are exactly 15 types of convex pentagons that can tile the plane monohedrally (Rao, 2017). A generic convex pentagon cannot tile the plane. Regular pentagons cannot tile the plane at all (interior angle 108° does not divide 360°).
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(copies of a convex pentagon) → COMPLETE(edge-to-edge tiling of the plane) FAILS → angle and edge constraints restrict to finitely many combinatorial types
known_resolutions: Use non-convex pentagons
Allow multiple tile types (Penrose tiles use two shapes)
Hyperbolic plane admits regular pentagon tilings

name: Impossibility of Periodicity for Certain Tile Sets
statement: There exist finite sets of tiles that can tile the plane but only aperiodically — no tiling by these tiles admits any translational symmetry. The recent Einstein 'hat' monotile (2023) achieves this with a single tile.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(tiles forced to tile the plane) → COMPLETE(periodic tiling) FAILS → local matching rules propagate hierarchical structure that is incompatible with any lattice periodicity
source: Berger (1966) first aperiodic set; Penrose (1974) two tiles; Smith et al. (2023) one tile
known_resolutions: Accept aperiodic order (quasicrystals)
Inflation/substitution rules describe the structure
Model sets from higher-dimensional lattices (cut-and-project)

name: Sphere Packing Density Bound
statement: No packing of equal spheres in R³ can exceed density π/(3√2) ≈ 0.7405 (the FCC/HCP density). The densest packing problem is solved in dimensions 1, 2, 3, 8, and 24; in most other dimensions, the optimal density is unknown.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(arrangement of equal spheres in R³) → COMPLETE(density > π/(3√2)) FAILS → Hales' proof by exhaustive case analysis and linear programming bounds
source: Kepler (1611 conjecture), Hales (1998/2005 proof, formally verified 2014)
known_resolutions: FCC and HCP achieve the bound
Allow non-congruent spheres
Different dimensions (E8 and Leech lattice are provably optimal in 8 and 24 dimensions)

name: Non-Freeness of Subgroups of Non-Free Groups
statement: Subgroups of free groups are always free (Nielsen-Schreier), but this fails for free abelian groups: subgroups of free abelian groups are free abelian, yet for general groups, subgroups can have wildly different structure. More critically: a finitely presented group can have undecidable word problem.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(generators + relations of a finitely presented group) → COMPLETE(decidable word problem) FAILS → Novikov-Boone theorem: there exist finitely presented groups whose word problem is undecidable
known_resolutions: Automatic groups have decidable word problem
Hyperbolic groups (Gromov) have linear-time word problem
Residually finite groups have solvable word problem in many cases

name: Galois Theory: Unsolvability of General Polynomial by Radicals
statement: A polynomial equation is solvable by radicals if and only if its Galois group is solvable. Since S_n is not solvable for n≥5, the generic polynomial of degree ≥5 is not solvable by radicals. This generalizes Abel-Ruffini with a precise criterion.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(field operations + n-th roots) → COMPLETE(root formula for degree ≥5) FAILS → Galois group S_n contains A_n which is simple and non-abelian for n≥5, blocking the solvable tower
source: Galois (1832, published 1846); Abel (1824) for impossibility; Galois for the complete characterization
known_resolutions: Bring radicals (special functions beyond n-th roots)
Tschirnhaus transformations reduce to Bring-Jerrard form
Theta functions and modular equations (Hermite, Kronecker)
Numerical methods (Newton-Raphson, etc.)

name: Burnside Problem (impossibility of finiteness from finite exponent)
statement: A finitely generated group of finite exponent need not be finite. For sufficiently large odd exponents n≥665, the free Burnside group B(m,n) on m≥2 generators is infinite.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(finitely many generators + relation g^n=1 for all g) → COMPLETE(finite group) FAILS → Novikov-Adian construction shows infinite groups satisfying the identity x^n=1
source: Burnside (1902 problem), Novikov-Adian (1968) negative solution for large odd exponents
known_resolutions: For small exponents (2,3,4,6) all such groups are finite
Restricted Burnside problem solved by Zelmanov (1994, Fields Medal)
Grigorchuk groups provide finitely generated infinite torsion groups with different properties

name: Impossibility of Classifying Wild Representations
statement: For algebras of wild representation type, a complete classification of indecomposable modules is impossible in a precise sense: the classification problem contains the problem of classifying pairs of matrices up to simultaneous conjugation, which is of 'wild' complexity.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(indecomposable modules over wild algebra) → COMPLETE(parametric classification) FAILS → wild algebras embed all finite-dimensional algebra representation problems, making classification undecidable in practice
known_resolutions: Tame algebras (including all Dynkin and extended Dynkin quivers) are fully classifiable
Auslander-Reiten theory gives structural information
Restrict to special subcategories

name: Non-existence of Finite Non-commutative Division Rings
statement: Every finite division ring is commutative (i.e., is a field). It is impossible to construct a finite skew field. Non-commutativity in division rings requires infinitely many elements.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(finite set + division ring axioms + non-commutativity) → COMPLETE(finite skew field) FAILS → class equation and cyclotomic polynomial argument forces center = whole ring
known_resolutions: Infinite division rings can be non-commutative (quaternions over R)
Finite near-fields relax some axioms
Octonions give non-associative alternative

name: Impossibility of Division Algebras Beyond Dimension 8
statement: The only finite-dimensional real division algebras are of dimension 1, 2, 4, or 8 (R, C, H, O). There is no real division algebra of dimension 3, 5, 6, 7, or any dimension >8.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(n-dimensional real vector space + bilinear multiplication without zero divisors) → COMPLETE(division algebra for n∉{1,2,4,8}) FAILS → topological K-theory (Bott periodicity) and Adams' theorem on Hopf invariants obstruct all other dimensions
source: Kervaire (1958), Bott-Milnor (1958), Adams (1960) via Hopf invariant one
known_resolutions: R (dim 1), C (dim 2), H (dim 4), O (dim 8) are the complete list
Clifford algebras generalize but are not division algebras
Lose properties: O loses associativity, sedenions lose alternativity and gain zero divisors

name: Cross Product Exists Only in Dimensions 0, 1, 3, and 7
statement: A bilinear cross product satisfying |a×b|² = |a|²|b|² - (a·b)² exists on R^n only for n=0,1,3,7. This is equivalent to the existence of normed division algebras in dimensions 1,2,4,8.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(bilinear antisymmetric product on R^n preserving norm) → COMPLETE(cross product for n∉{0,1,3,7}) FAILS → parallelizability of S^n and Hopf invariant one theorem restrict dimensions
source: Follows from Adams' theorem on Hopf invariant one (1960)
known_resolutions: Generalized cross products in higher dimensions lose some properties
Exterior algebra (wedge product) works in all dimensions but lives in ΛR^n
Lie brackets provide alternatives

name: Unsolvability of the Entscheidungsproblem
statement: There is no algorithm that takes a first-order sentence as input and determines in finite time whether the sentence is universally valid (true in every structure). The decision problem for first-order predicate logic is undecidable.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(first-order logic + decision algorithm) → COMPLETE(validity oracle) FAILS → Church and Turing independently showed reduction from halting problem
known_resolutions: Propositional logic is decidable (SAT is NP-complete but decidable)
Monadic first-order logic is decidable
Decidable fragments: two-variable logic, guarded fragment, EPR/Bernays-Schönfinkel

name: Goodstein's Theorem (unprovable in Peano Arithmetic)
statement: Every Goodstein sequence eventually reaches 0. This is a true statement about natural numbers that cannot be proved in Peano Arithmetic, demonstrating a concrete incompleteness.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(Peano Arithmetic axioms) → COMPLETE(proof of Goodstein's theorem) FAILS → requires ordinal induction up to ε₀, which exceeds PA's proof-theoretic ordinal
source: Goodstein (1944) theorem, Kirby & Paris (1982) independence from PA
known_resolutions: Provable in ZFC using transfinite induction
Provable in PA + transfinite induction up to ε₀
Gentzen's consistency proof operates at exactly this ordinal level

name: Paris-Harrington Theorem (unprovable Ramsey variant in PA)
statement: The Paris-Harrington strengthening of the finite Ramsey theorem (requiring the monochromatic set to be 'relatively large') is true but unprovable in Peano Arithmetic.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(PA axioms + finite combinatorial statement) → COMPLETE(proof of Paris-Harrington) FAILS → the function grows faster than any provably total function of PA, exceeding PA's proof-theoretic strength
known_resolutions: Provable in ZFC
Provable in PA + Σ₁-soundness
First 'natural' mathematical statement shown independent of PA

name: Exotic Structures on R⁴
statement: R⁴ admits uncountably many pairwise non-diffeomorphic smooth structures. This is unique to dimension 4: for all other dimensions n≠4, R^n has a unique smooth structure (up to diffeomorphism).
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(smooth structure on R⁴) → COMPLETE(unique smooth type) FAILS → Donaldson/Freedman: intersection form constraints in dimension 4 create gap between topological and smooth categories
source: Donaldson (1983), Freedman (1982); exotic R⁴ constructed by Gompf, Taubes (1985+)
known_resolutions: Topologically all are standard R⁴
Unique in all other dimensions
Gauge theory and Seiberg-Witten invariants detect the exotic structures

name: Whitney Embedding Theorem (dimension bound is tight)
statement: Every smooth n-manifold can be smoothly embedded in R^(2n), but not always in R^(2n-1). The real projective plane RP² cannot be embedded in R³ (only immersed). The bound 2n is sharp.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(smooth n-manifold + lower-dimensional Euclidean space) → COMPLETE(smooth embedding in R^(2n-1) for all manifolds) FAILS → characteristic class obstructions (e.g., non-orientability of RP²) prevent lower-dimensional embedding
known_resolutions: 2n dimensions always suffice
Many manifolds embed in lower dimensions than 2n
Immersions (allowing self-intersection) need only 2n-1 by Whitney

name: Non-smoothability of Topological Manifolds
statement: Not every topological manifold admits a smooth structure. Kervaire's 10-dimensional manifold has no smooth structure at all. In dimension 4, Freedman's E8 manifold is a topological 4-manifold with no smooth structure.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(topological manifold) → COMPLETE(smooth/differentiable structure) FAILS → Rokhlin's theorem and Donaldson's theorem obstruct smoothing certain intersection forms
source: Kervaire (1960) 10-dim example; Freedman (1982) / Donaldson (1983) in dimension 4
known_resolutions: PL (piecewise-linear) structures as intermediate
Dimensions ≤3: topological = smooth = PL (Moise)
Surgery theory classifies when smoothing is possible

name: Dehn's Theorem (Hilbert's Third Problem)
statement: It is impossible to cut a regular tetrahedron into finitely many polyhedral pieces and reassemble them into a cube of equal volume. Scissors-congruence in 3D requires equal Dehn invariants, not just equal volume.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(finite polyhedral dissection of tetrahedron) → COMPLETE(reassembly into cube) FAILS → Dehn invariant (edge lengths tensor dihedral angles in R⊗(R/Q)) differs between tetrahedron and cube
known_resolutions: In 2D, any two equal-area polygons are scissors-congruent (Bolyai-Gerwien)
Allow curved cuts
Sydler (1965): volume + Dehn invariant completely characterize 3D scissors-congruence

name: Euler's Polyhedron Formula as Obstruction
statement: Any convex polyhedron must satisfy V - E + F = 2. It is impossible to construct a convex polyhedron violating this relation. More generally, the Euler characteristic is a topological invariant that obstructs certain combinatorial configurations.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(vertices, edges, faces of convex polyhedron) → COMPLETE(V-E+F ≠ 2) FAILS → Euler characteristic χ=2 for S² is a homotopy invariant that constrains all CW-decompositions
source: Euler (1758), rigorous proof by Cauchy and Legendre
known_resolutions: Non-convex polyhedra can have different Euler characteristic (toroidal: V-E+F=0)
Generalization to CW-complexes and higher dimensions
Gauss-Bonnet theorem connects to curvature

name: Russell's Paradox (impossibility of unrestricted comprehension)
statement: The set of all sets that do not contain themselves both contains and does not contain itself. Unrestricted set comprehension {x : φ(x)} for arbitrary predicates φ is inconsistent.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(unrestricted comprehension axiom) → COMPLETE(consistent set theory) FAILS → R = {x : x∉x} yields R∈R ↔ R∉R, a contradiction
known_resolutions: ZFC restricts comprehension to separation (subset) axiom
Type theory (Russell's own solution)
NBG class theory distinguishes sets and proper classes
Paraconsistent set theories

name: Impossibility of Uniformly Approximating Discontinuous Functions by Continuous Ones
statement: The uniform limit of continuous functions is continuous. Therefore, a discontinuous function (like the Dirichlet function) cannot be the uniform limit of any sequence of continuous functions. The space C(X) is closed in the sup-norm topology.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(sequence of continuous functions + uniform convergence) → COMPLETE(discontinuous limit) FAILS → ε/3 argument: uniform convergence preserves continuity
known_resolutions: Pointwise convergence can produce discontinuous limits (Baire class 1)
L^p convergence allows discontinuous limits
Distributions extend beyond classical functions

name: Heawood Conjecture / Map Coloring on Surfaces
statement: On a surface of Euler characteristic χ (other than the sphere), the maximum number of colors needed to color any map is exactly ⌊(7+√(49-24χ))/2⌋. The Klein bottle is the sole exception (needs 6, not the predicted 7).
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(map on surface of genus g + fewer than Heawood number of colors) → COMPLETE(proper coloring) FAILS → Euler characteristic constrains maximum complete graph embeddable on the surface
source: Heawood (1890) upper bound, Ringel & Youngs (1968) complete proof
known_resolutions: Sphere case is the Four Color Theorem (special and harder)
Klein bottle is a known exception
Graph minor theory (Robertson-Seymour) provides alternative framework

name: Szemerédi Regularity Lemma Tower Bound
statement: The Szemerédi regularity lemma requires a number of parts that grows as a tower of exponentials in 1/ε. Gowers showed this tower-type bound is necessary: no polynomial or even iterated-exponential bound suffices for the general lemma.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(ε-regular partition of dense graph) → COMPLETE(bounded number of parts) FAILS → Gowers' lower bound: there exist graphs requiring tower(1/ε) parts for ε-regularity
source: Szemerédi (1975) lemma, Gowers (1997) tower lower bound
known_resolutions: Frieze-Kannan weak regularity has polynomial bounds (weaker guarantee)
Arithmetic regularity lemma for abelian groups
Accept the tower as intrinsic to the combinatorial structure

name: Gibbard-Satterthwaite Theorem
statement: Every voting rule for three or more candidates that is surjective and strategy-proof (immune to tactical voting) must be dictatorial. Non-trivial strategy-proof social choice is impossible.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(surjectivity + strategy-proofness + non-dictatorship) → COMPLETE(voting rule for ≥3 candidates) FAILS → manipulation is always possible unless one voter dictates the outcome
known_resolutions: Restrict to two candidates (majority rule works)
Single-peaked preferences allow strategy-proof mechanisms (median voter)
Randomized mechanisms (Gibbard 1977)
Accept bounded manipulation

name: No Free Lunch Theorem (Wolpert-Macready)
statement: Averaged over all possible objective functions, all optimization algorithms perform equally. No search/optimization algorithm is universally superior to any other, including random search.
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(optimization algorithm + all possible cost functions) → COMPLETE(universally superior performance) FAILS → permutation symmetry of the set of all functions means any algorithm's gains on some functions are exactly offset by losses on others
known_resolutions: Restrict to structured function classes (convex, Lipschitz, etc.)
Bayesian optimization with priors
Problem-specific algorithm design
Algorithmic information theory: Kolmogorov complexity provides a universal prior
